symbols: floodwaters — divine judgment and the cleansing destruction of a corrupt world, echoing the Genesis Flood
submerged machine — human technology and pride swallowed by forces beyond human control
organic tendrils overtaking the vehicle — nature reclaiming what was made by human hands, the futility of human works
bare winter trees — dormancy and desolation, the lifelessness of a judged world
still water at dusk — ominous silence after catastrophe, the calm of completed judgment
description: A digital painting depicts a dusky winter lakeshore. In the center of still, dark floodwater, a car is partially submerged, its red taillights glowing beneath the surface. Strange organic tendrils or root-like growths wrap around and over the vehicle, as though the water and vegetation are consuming it. Bare, skeletal tree branches frame the scene on both sides. The sky is a cold grey-blue with a faint lighter band at the horizon where distant hills are visible. The water reflects the vehicle and the dim light.
theological_description: Though a work of contemporary science fiction art, the title 'Things from the Flood' and the imagery of a world submerged invoke the most catastrophic divine judgment in Scripture: the Flood of Noah (Genesis 6-9). In the biblical narrative, God saw that the wickedness of humanity was great and that every intention of the heart was 'only evil continually' (Gen 6:5), and He resolved to blot out the corrupted creation with water. The submerged machine, overtaken by organic growth, evokes the futility of human technological ambition against divine sovereignty -- echoing the Tower of Babel's hubris (Gen 11:1-9). The eerie stillness of the flooded landscape recalls the moment when 'the waters prevailed so mightily on the earth that all the high mountains under the whole heaven were covered' (Gen 7:19). Peter draws on the Flood as a type of eschatological judgment: 'the world that then existed was deluged with water and perished' and warns that the present heavens and earth 'are stored up for fire' (2 Pet 3:6-7). The scene serves as a modern memento of divine judgment on a world that has forgotten its Creator.

scripture_refs.reason: God's decision to destroy the corrupt earth with a flood -- the direct biblical event evoked by the title and imagery.
The waters prevailing over the earth, covering everything -- the visual reality depicted in this flooded landscape.
Peter's use of the Noahic Flood as a type of eschatological judgment by fire, linking past and future catastrophe.
The Tower of Babel -- human technological pride brought low by divine intervention, echoed in the submerged machine.
Isaiah's apocalyptic vision of the earth laid waste and defiled under its inhabitants -- resonant with the desolate landscape.
